author: @RWBHere
text:
The penny might have just dropped, but clarification is needed: 

Are you making a Hydrogen - Oxygen fuel cell in reverse? I.e, sunlight and temperature cause a reaction (photosynthesis?), releasing Hydrogen and Oxygen, etc,  and this causes the potential difference between the anode and cathode?

Or is the process more like: The microbes and plants, plus sunlight and warmth, releasing the gases. _And then the gases recombine to create the PD?_ Call it a full-cycle fuel cell, or a holistic fuel cell, if you like.

Which process is going on here, please? Or is it something else? Thanks Robert.

author: @RWBHere
text:
That's awesome! You've improved on the design by at least an order of magnitude, maybe three. And it's what, 10 cm square? If you can make a square metre of it, then that's another 10,000-fold increase, giving, let's say an instantaneous 100 Watts or so, for around 6 seconds. Go to a hectare per cell, on otherwise unusable ground, or rooftops, etc, and you're beginning to generate really useful power with minimal materials cost, and for as long as there is water and daylight. Maybe even during darkness. Enclose it, so that the water is recycled, reducing cost. Then with further development of the idea, you should be able to multiply output by at least another order of magnitude. 

The questions are, 'What are the stumbling blocks which have stopped people from making this a viable idea?' 

The efficiency might be low, at present, but it can be constructed on any otherwise unused surface on which some light and warmth exists; places where it's difficult to put PV panels or heat exchanger panels. Furthermore, with development, efficiency should be improved upon.

And, 'How much does it cost, and can it be made to return the investment?' 

That's partly answered by the above, but the rest is unknown right now, and there's little to stop private individuals from making their own systems.

Then 'What continuous Voltage and Current is generated per square metre?'

Investigations are certainly needed here.

'What relationship is there between light levels, light spectrum and temperature?'

Ditto.

Finally, 'Is this an effect of the PD between the anode and cathode collectors, with the electrolyte, rather than the result of a redox reaction between microbes and plants?'

That's something to be investigated. (Time to consult the interwebs.) 

Thanks Robert!

author: @nigeljohnson9820
text:
This is very interesting, but rather than using it to generate power, can it be use to measure the health of the plant?
Is it possible to build plant pots that can report back the health of the plants it contains?
It would be useful to report water level, pH etc. Even better if the power output can correlated with the rate of plant metabolism, indicating the vigor of the plant.
Consider the value of a plant pot, to which could be connected and IoT or LCD device that notifies the user of the plants needs.

author: @zhafblaze
text: Last 3 years, I had participated in Technology and Science Expo at my university and presented my project about microbial fuel cell. My project used 3 plants which were Pandan that can be found widely in my country,carbon electrodes and water pump.The setup is similar like electrolysis except we put separate water pump to give oxygen to the plants that were buried in wet soil. The plant battery were short circuit in series and we got around 1 volts if I'm not mistaken. Luckily, our project win the best functionality award as it suited the green engineering theme. After I discovered your video, I realized that I can develop my project to harvest more energy with less cost.  Thank you Robert for the nice video. Looking forward to see you use graphene electrode in MFC.

author: @henrid6881
text:
Chapeau Bruce !!! I initially found your first videos by looking into MFCs, because of your experiences with activated carbon and later on the membranes.

This is great , I am so excited you are looking into this technology. MFCs as it goes hand and hand with all carbon based Super Caps.
Of course people might have figured it out that we are talking about long term slow charging  energy storage with the ability to quickly discharge. 

Bruce Logan MFC is a great start, plus he has videos .  People can then look at the references. 
Microbial Fuel Cells - MIT
PDFhttps://web.mit.edu › SoBEI › Info_files


The Chinese are patenting the heck out of this tech. This tech started with trying to find a way to rejuvenate the aging wastewater purifying plants in the US. 

So you see my hypothesis is that the membrane could be replaced by a fiber suspended ionic liquid membrane called SLIMs with a carbon fiber GO coated anode  and a stainless steel mesh type cathode. Ideally the IL should be a NaCl alginate with a chiral chitosantype permaganate manganese  compound to help electron motility which decreases the internal resistance and increases power density 
Hopefully nothing with fluor to make as environmentaly friendly product. 
I haven't seen any research done in this way in prior art. 

There is an increasingly growing body of knowledge being formed in this field. The most interesting research is yet to come. 

Ohhhhh Robert bless you man !!! 
I hope one day Ill be able to meet you . 

Your doing the humanity a big service by diffusing and vulgarising MFCs and the underlying tech.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It is an exciting tech mate and I agree most of the best work is yet to come - I also agree that spreading the word in a 'user-friendly' way is a key aspect - cheers

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text: Plants digest minerals by injecting protons and free electrons directly into the soil, I have always wondered how hard it would be to capture, store and use these positively and negatively charged ions as they come out of a plant.

replies.author: @flashlit123
replies.text: pranjal kumar there is a commercial product called a MudWatt that produces a very small amount of electricity that flashes an LED or runs a clock.   Took a few days for the microbes to multiply enough to start producing electricity.  Basically all it is is a couple carbon felt electrodes.  One buried in the mud and one on the surface exposed to air but still kept moist.  The voltage is pretty low so you either need a circuit to increase the voltage or multiple devices in series.

author: @gerryjamesedwards1227
text: Very interesting! Try adding a tiny fraction of a non-ionic surfactant to the water. I've been growing plants with 100ppm Polysorbate 20 (P20, or stearic acid is derived from coconut oil and 100% organic sources are available) and seeing amazing results. I came across a 2008 paper by Yang called "The effects of non-ionic surfactants on plant growth" which found beneficial effects at this very narrow, and tiny, concentration of what is one of the most widely used chemicals in agriculture (as a wetting agent in arid climates but used in far greater concentrations, at which all the beneficial, synergistic effects disappear). These effects start from germination with a marked increase in size and vigour of the meristem, but the most applicable to what you're working on are the ~400% reduction in moisture loss through transpiration, and improved transfer of ions across the cell membranes in the roots apparently due to changes in the cell itself as well as the greater oxygen availability at the roots from the reduced surface tension. As a side effect, things like viruses, fungal spores and many insect eggs are unable to to get a foot hold on a plant fed with P20, as they become water-logged and die.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thank you mate - i just ordred some and i wil give it a go - cheers

replies.author: @gerryjamesedwards1227
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering sorry, but another thing while I think of it, did you hear about the graphene microphone diaphragm that was tried fairly recently? It was adapted from a Bruel & Kjaer measurement mic capsule, a medium-diaphragm condenser type, in a most McGiver like manner, by getting a sheet of graphene on nickel and etching away all the nickel with ferric-chloride and scooping up the graphene onto a plastic frame. A frequency response approaching 1 MHz with very competitive sensitivity, even without exploring the possibilities fully with regards to the tensile strength and electronic properties. The sensors that this substance is going to enable in the future will be amazing things!

replies.text: this is different mate - i should have pointed that out - in a potatoe or lemon battery a zinc rod and a copper rod are pushed into the potatoe or lemon and it is the difference in redox potential of zinc and copper in acid environments that drive the reaction and poison the potatoe or lemon - here the process happens in nature notrmally and there is no metal to rot!

author: @trevorvanzuydam8115
text: Can't wait for the live event. Thanks for sharing

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate
